title: Learning, Teaching and Applying Geometry and Handling Data

sessions.title: Straight Lines
indicators: Name a given straight line using block letters
Name a given angle
Identify the types of angles
sessions.content:
What is a Straight Line?

A straight line is a line such that, if any portion of it is placed with its ends in the line, the entire portion so placed will lie in the line, however it may be applied.

A straight line is also called a right line. The word 'line', unqualified, is understood to mean straight line.

Angles

An angle is the figure formed by two straight lines which diverge from a point or converge to a point. The point is the vertex of the angle and the lines are its sides (arms of the angles).

Designating angles:

  • By a number placed within it (e.g., angle a, angle b)
  • By three letters – the letter at the vertex being written in the middle (e.g., angle ABC or CBA)
  • Example: Angle ABC can be written as \(\angle ABC\)

Adjacent angles: Two angles are adjacent if they have a common vertex and a common side between them.

Types of Angles

Right angle (90°): One-quarter of a whole turn – the angle made by a horizontal line and vertical line.

Acute angle: An angle that is less than a right angle (< 90°).

Obtuse angle: An angle that is greater than a right angle but less than two right angles (90° < angle < 180°).

Reflex angle: An angle that is greater than two right angles but less than four right angles (180° < angle < 360°).

Complementary angles: Two angles whose sum is 90° (a right angle).

Supplementary angles: Two angles whose sum is 180° (two right angles).

Vertically opposite angles: Two angles whose sides are extensions of each other – they are equal. In Fig 1.9, angles \(a\) and \(c\) are vertically opposite, and angles \(b\) and \(d\) are vertically opposite.

sessions.title: Angles at a Point
indicators: Find out that angles at a point sum up to 360°
sessions.content:
Angles at a Point

Angles at a point add up to 360°. Together they make one revolution.

Example 1: Find the value of \(x\) in the diagram where angles are \(4x - 30\), \(x + 10\), \(x\), and \(90°\).

Solution:

\(4x - 30 + x + 10 + x + 90 = 360\)

\(6x + 70 = 360\)

\(6x = 290\)

\(x = 48.33°\)

Example 2: Find the values of the angles marked by letters where angles are \(3y\), \(2y\), \(2y\), \(y\), and \(108°\).

\(3y + 2y + 2y + y + 108 = 360\)

\(8y + 108 = 360\)

\(8y = 252\)

\(y = 31.5°\)

Angles Properties of Parallel Lines

Parallel lines: Two lines in a plane which do not meet no matter how far they are extended. When line AB is parallel to CD, we write AB // CD.

A transversal line is a line which cuts a pair of lines.

1. Corresponding Angles: Angles in the same position at the point of intersection of the transversal line – they are equal.

  • \(\angle a = \angle e\)
  • \(\angle b = \angle f\)
  • \(\angle c = \angle g\)
  • \(\angle d = \angle h\)

2. Alternate Angles: Angles on opposite sides of the transversal – they are equal.

  • \(\angle b = \angle f\)
  • \(\angle c = \angle e\)

3. Co-interior Angles: Angles on the same side of the transversal between the parallel lines – they sum up to 180°.

  • \(\angle b + \angle e = 180°\)
  • \(\angle c + \angle f = 180°\)

sessions.title: Angle Properties of a Triangle
indicators: Identify the types of triangles and their properties
sessions.content:
Triangle

A triangle is a simple closed curve created by three line-segments. Any three non-collinear points form a unique triangle.

Basic elements: Sides, angles, and vertices.

Interior angles: Angles inside the shape – the sum of the interior angles of a triangle is 180°.

  • \(\angle a + \angle b + \angle c = 180°\)

Exterior angle: An angle formed by extending one side of the triangle – the exterior angle is equal to the sum of the two opposite interior angles.

  • \(\text{Exterior angle} = \text{Opposite interior angle 1} + \text{Opposite interior angle 2}\)

Types of Triangles

1. Equilateral Triangle

  • All sides are equal in length
  • All interior angles are equal (60° each)
  • Has three lines of symmetry
  • Has rotational symmetry of order 3

2. Isosceles Triangle

  • Two sides are equal
  • Two interior angles are equal (base angles)
  • Has one line of symmetry
  • No rotational symmetry

3. Scalene Triangle

  • All sides are different in length
  • All interior angles are different
  • No line symmetry
  • No rotational symmetry

4. Obtuse Angled Triangle

  • One interior angle is more than 90°

5. Acute Angled Triangle

  • All interior angles are less than 90°

6. Right Angled Triangle

  • One interior angle is equal to 90°

sessions.title: Quadrilaterals
indicators: Identify types of quadrilaterals and their properties
sessions.content:
Quadrilateral

A quadrilateral is a polygon with four edges (sides) and four vertices or corners. The interior angles of a simple quadrilateral add up to 360°.

Types of Quadrilaterals and Their Properties

1. Rectangle

  • Each angle is 90°
  • Two pairs of opposite sides are equal
  • Opposite sides are parallel
  • Rotational symmetry of order 2
  • Diagonals bisect each other and are equal in length

2. Square

  • All sides are equal
  • Opposite sides are parallel and equal
  • Four lines of symmetry
  • Diagonals are equal in length and they bisect each other at right angles
  • Rotational symmetry of order 4

3. Rhombus

  • All sides are equal
  • Opposite sides are parallel
  • Opposite angles are equal
  • Has two lines of symmetry
  • Rotational symmetry of order 2
  • Diagonals bisect each other at right angles

4. Parallelogram

  • Opposite angles are equal
  • Two pairs of opposite sides that are equal and parallel
  • Diagonals divide each other into two equal parts
  • Rotational symmetry of order 2

5. Kite

  • Two pairs of adjacent sides are equal
  • Each pair of opposite angles equal
  • One diagonal as line of symmetry
  • No rotational symmetry
  • Only one diagonal is bisected
  • The diagonals cross at right angle

6. Trapezium

  • One pair of parallel sides
  • Can have a line symmetry if the sides connecting the two parallel sides are equal
  • No rotational symmetry

sessions.title: Solid Shapes
indicators: Identify types of solid shapes and their properties
Identify the faces, edges and vertices of solid shapes
Use Euler's formula to find the number of faces, edges or vertices of solid shapes
sessions.content:
Solid Shapes

A solid is a polyhedron if it is made up of only polygonal faces. The faces meet at edges which are line segments, and the edges meet at points called vertices.

3-D shapes/objects are those which do not lie completely in a plane. They have different views from different positions.

Common solids: Cuboid, Cube, Cylinder, Cone, Rectangular pyramid, Triangular prism, Sphere.

Nets of Solids

A geometry net is a two-dimensional shape that can be folded to form a three-dimensional shape or a solid.

Steps to determine if a net forms a solid:

  • Ensure the solid and the net have the same number of faces
  • Match the shapes of the faces of the solid with the corresponding faces in the net
  • Visualize how the net is to be folded to form the solid
  • Ensure all sides fit together properly

Nets are useful in finding the surface area of solids.

Faces, Vertices and Edges

Faces: Each surface of a solid

Edges: Where two faces meet

Vertices: The point or corner where the edges meet

Euler's Formula: For any polyhedron, V + F = E + 2 (where V = vertices, F = faces, E = edges)

Examples:

  • Cuboid: 8 vertices, 12 edges, 6 faces \(\Rightarrow V + F = 8 + 6 = 14 = E + 2 = 12 + 2\)
  • Triangular prism: 6 vertices, 9 edges, 5 faces \(\Rightarrow V + F = 6 + 5 = 11 = E + 2 = 9 + 2\)
  • Rectangular pyramid: 5 vertices, 8 edges, 5 faces \(\Rightarrow V + F = 5 + 5 = 10 = E + 2 = 8 + 2\)
  • Cube: 8 vertices, 12 edges, 6 faces \(\Rightarrow V + F = 8 + 6 = 14 = E + 2 = 12 + 2\)
  • Tetrahedron: 4 vertices, 6 edges, 4 faces \(\Rightarrow V + F = 4 + 4 = 8 = E + 2 = 6 + 2\)

sessions.title: Congruence and Similarities
indicators: Identify whether two given figures are congruent
Identify the number of lines of symmetry of two and three dimensional shapes
sessions.content:
Congruent Triangles

Congruent triangles have both the same shape and the same size. They have the same angle measures and the same side lengths.

Triangle congruence criteria:

  • SSS (Side-Side-Side): All three pairs of corresponding sides are equal
  • SAS (Side-Angle-Side): Two pairs of corresponding sides and the corresponding angles between them are equal
  • ASA (Angle-Side-Angle): Two pairs of corresponding angles and the corresponding sides between them are equal
  • AAS (Angle-Angle-Side): Two pairs of corresponding angles and one pair of corresponding sides (not between the angles) are equal
  • RHS (Right angle-Hypotenuse-Side): The pair of hypotenuses and another pair of corresponding sides are equal in two right triangles

Note: Congruent triangles are also similar.

Similar Triangles

Similar triangles have the same shape, but not necessarily the same size. They have the same angle measures, but not the same side lengths.

Key points:

  • Corresponding sides are in the same ratio
  • Corresponding angles are equal
  • If two objects are congruent, then they are also similar

Lines of Symmetry

Symmetry: When folded, the two sides overlap and cover each other perfectly. The 'mirror' or 'fold' line that gives these two equal reflections is called the line of symmetry.

Regular polygons and lines of symmetry:

  • Square: 4 lines of symmetry
  • Rectangle: 2 lines of symmetry
  • Equilateral triangle: 3 lines of symmetry
  • Isosceles triangle: 1 line of symmetry
  • Scalene triangle: 0 lines of symmetry
  • Kite: 1 line of symmetry
  • Rhombus: 2 lines of symmetry
  • Parallelogram: 0 lines of symmetry
  • Regular pentagon: 5 lines of symmetry
  • Regular hexagon: 6 lines of symmetry

Rotational Symmetry

Rotational symmetry: If we rotate an object and it looks the same again. The number of times it looks the same in one full rotation is the order of rotational symmetry.

  • Square: Rotational symmetry of order 4
  • Rectangle: Rotational symmetry of order 2
  • Equilateral triangle: Rotational symmetry of order 3
  • Star of David: Rotational symmetry of order 6
  • Circle: Rotational symmetry of infinite order

sessions.title: Construction of Perpendiculars and Parallel Lines
indicators: Construct perpendicular bisector of a given line segment
Construct perpendicular to the line passing through a point on the line
Construct perpendicular to a line passing through a point not on the line
Construct parallel lines
sessions.content:
1. Bisection of a Straight Line

Steps:

  • Draw a straight line \(AB\) of any convenient length
  • With compass point at \(A\), draw an arc above and below the line (radius more than half of AB)
  • With same radius and \(B\) as centre, draw arcs above and below the line to intersect with the first arcs at \(P\) and \(M\)
  • Join \(P\) to \(M\) with a ruler
  • \(PM\) is the perpendicular bisector of \(AB\)

Properties: \(PM \perp AB\) and \(AM = MB\)

2. Construction of a Perpendicular at a Point on a Given Line

Steps:

  • Draw a line \(AB\) and mark a point \(M\) on it
  • With \(M\) as centre, draw a semi-circle around M to cut AB at \(P\) and \(Q\)
  • With compass point at \(P\), draw an arc on one side of the line
  • Using same radius and centre at \(Q\), draw an arc to intersect the first arc at \(X\)
  • Join \(X\) and \(M\) – line \(MX\) is perpendicular to AB at M

3. Construction of a Perpendicular to a Line from a Point Outside the Line

Steps:

  • Draw a line \(AB\) and indicate a point \(M\) outside the line
  • With \(M\) as centre, draw arcs to cut \(AB\) at \(A\) and \(B\)
  • With compass at \(A\), draw an arc on the other side of the line
  • Using \(B\) as centre and same radius, draw an arc to intersect the first arc at \(K\)
  • Join \(M\) and \(K\) – \(MK\) is the perpendicular to AB from M

4. Construction of a Parallel Line to a Given Line

Steps:

  • Draw line \(PQ\)
  • Mark two points \(A\) and \(B\) on \(PQ\)
  • With \(A\) as centre, draw a semi-circle above \(PQ\)
  • Using same radius and centre at \(B\), draw another semi-circle
  • Draw a line to touch the turning points of the two semi-circles at \(R\) and \(S\)
  • \(RS\) is parallel to \(PQ\) (i.e., \(RS \parallel PQ\))

sessions.title: Construction of Angles
indicators: Construct angles using a pair of compasses and a ruler only
sessions.content:
Construction of 60°

  • Draw line \(PQ\)
  • With \(P\) as centre, draw a semi-circle around P to cut PQ at \(X\)
  • Using same radius and compass point at \(X\), draw an arc to intersect the first arc at \(Y\)
  • Join \(P\) to \(Y\) – \(\angle YPQ = 60°\)

Construction of 30°

  • First construct a 60° angle
  • Bisect the 60° angle by drawing arcs from \(X\) and \(Y\) to intersect at \(A\)
  • Join \(P\) to \(A\) – \(\angle APQ = 30°\)

Construction of 120°

  • Draw line \(PQ\)
  • With \(P\) as centre, draw a semi-circle to cut PQ at \(M\) and \(N\)
  • With \(N\) as centre, draw an arc to cut the semi-circle at \(A\)
  • With \(A\) as centre, draw another arc to cut the semi-circle at \(V\)
  • Join \(P\) to \(V\) – \(\angle VPQ = 120°\)

Construction of 90°

  • Draw line \(PQ\) with extension at \(P\)
  • With \(P\) as centre, draw a semi-circle to cut PQ at \(N\) and \(M\)
  • With \(N\) and \(M\) as centres, draw arcs to cut the semi-circle at \(A\) and \(B\)
  • With \(A\) and \(B\) as centres, draw arcs to intersect at \(V\)
  • Join \(V\) to \(P\) – \(\angle VPQ = 90°\)

Construction of 135°

  • Construct a 90° angle (\(\angle VPB\))
  • Bisect the remaining angle to get 45°
  • \(90° + 45° = 135°\)

Construction of 15°

  • Construct a 30° angle
  • Bisect the 30° angle to get 15°

sessions.title: Construction of Triangles
indicators: Construct triangles using a ruler and a pair of compasses only
sessions.content:
Construction of Triangles Given the Length of All Three Sides

  • Construct line \(AB\) of given length
  • With \(A\) as centre and radius \(AC\), draw an arc above the line
  • With \(B\) as centre and radius \(BC\), draw an arc to intersect the first arc at \(C\)
  • Join \(A\) to \(C\) and \(B\) to \(C\) to complete triangle ABC

Construction of Triangles with Three Different Sides

  • Draw the longest side (or any of the three sides) as the base
  • With one end as centre and the length of one side as radius, draw an arc
  • With the other end as centre and the length of the third side as radius, draw an arc to intersect the first
  • Join the intersection point to both ends to complete the triangle

Construction of a Triangle Given the Length of Two Sides and the Angle Between Them (SAS)

  • Draw the base line
  • Construct the given angle at one end
  • Measure the second side along the angle
  • Join to complete the triangle

Construction of a Triangle Given Two Interior Angles and a Side (ASA)

  • Draw the given side as the base
  • Construct the two given angles at each end of the base
  • The two angle lines will intersect to form the third vertex

Construction of a Right-Angled Triangle Given Hypotenuse and One Side (RHS)

  • Draw the hypotenuse as the base
  • Construct a perpendicular at one end
  • With the other end as centre and the given side as radius, draw an arc to intersect the perpendicular
  • Join to complete the triangle

sessions.title: Construction of Quadrilaterals and Hexagon
indicators: Construct quadrilaterals and hexagons using a ruler and a pair of compasses only
sessions.content:
Construction of a Square

  • Construct a line of the given side length
  • Construct perpendiculars at both ends
  • Measure the side length along each perpendicular
  • Join the two points to complete the square

Construction of a Rectangle

  • Construct a line of the given length
  • Construct perpendiculars at both ends
  • Measure the given breadth along each perpendicular
  • Join the two points to complete the rectangle

Construction of a Parallelogram

  • Draw the base line
  • Construct the given angle at one end
  • Measure the adjacent side along the angle
  • Using the same lengths, construct the opposite sides

Construction of a Rhombus

  • Draw the base line
  • Construct the given angle
  • Measure the side length along both lines
  • Construct the opposite side parallel to the base

Construction of a Regular Hexagon (Side = 4cm)

  • Draw a circle with centre \(O\) and radius equal to the side length (4cm)
  • Make a point \(A\) on the circumference
  • With the same radius (4cm), mark points \(B, C, D, E, F\) on the circumference
  • Join all points to form the regular hexagon \(ABCDEF\)

sessions.title: Loci
indicators: Construct a locus
Construct circumscribed and inscribed circles
sessions.content:
What is a Locus?

The locus of a point is the path traced by the point as it moves under certain specified conditions.

Types of Loci

1. Circle

  • A locus of points which is equidistant from one point (the centre)
  • If \(OA = r\), the locus of \(A\) is a circle with radius \(r\)

2. The locus of points equidistant from two fixed points

  • The path traced out by a point which is always equidistant from two fixed points
  • This is the perpendicular bisector of the line segment with given endpoints

3. The locus of points equidistant from two lines that intersect at a point

  • If \(M\) is such that it is equidistant from the lines \(PQ\) and \(PR\), the locus of \(M\) is the angle bisector of the angle formed by the two lines

Circumscribed Circle

  • A circle that passes through the three vertices of a triangle
  • To construct: Bisect any two sides of the triangle
  • The point of intersection of the bisectors is the centre of the circle
  • The radius is the distance from the centre to any vertex

Inscribed Circle

  • A circle that touches all three sides of a triangle
  • To construct: Bisect any two angles of the triangle
  • The point of intersection of the bisectors is the centre of the circle
  • The radius is the perpendicular distance from the centre to any side

sessions.title: The Right-Angled Triangle
indicators: Identify the sides of a right-angled triangle
Develop Pythagoras Theorem
Use Pythagoras Theorem in real life situations
sessions.content:
Right-Angled Triangle

A right-angled triangle is a triangle with one interior angle equal to \(90°\).

Labelling sides:

  • Hypotenuse: The longest side – always opposite the right angle
  • Opposite: The side opposite the angle marked \(\theta\)
  • Adjacent: The third side, adjacent to the indicated angle \(\theta\)

Pythagoras Theorem

In a right-angled triangle, the square of the hypotenuse is equal to the sum of the squares of the other two sides.

Formula: \(c^2 = a^2 + b^2\)

  • Finding hypotenuse: \(c = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}\)
  • Finding one leg: \(a = \sqrt{c^2 - b^2}\)
  • Finding the other leg: \(b = \sqrt{c^2 - a^2}\)

Example 1: A rectangle has a length of 15cm and breadth of 8cm. Find the length of its diagonal.

\(d^2 = 15^2 + 8^2 = 225 + 64 = 289\)

\(d = \sqrt{289} = 17\) cm

Example 2: A ladder leans against a vertical wall to a height of 15m. If the foot of the ladder is 8m away from the wall, calculate the length of the ladder.

\(l^2 = 15^2 + 8^2 = 225 + 64 = 289\)

\(l = \sqrt{289} = 17\) m

Pythagorean Triple (Triad)

When the three lengths of a right-angled triangle are all whole numbers, they are called a Pythagorean triple.

Most common: 3, 4, 5

Examples based on 3, 4, 5:

  • 6, 8, 10 (multiply by 2)
  • 9, 12, 15 (multiply by 3)
  • 12, 16, 20 (multiply by 4)
  • 15, 20, 25 (multiply by 5)

Other triples: 5, 12, 13; 8, 15, 17; 7, 24, 25

sessions.title: Trigonometric Ratios of a Right-Angled Triangle
indicators: Use trigonometric ratios to solve problems
sessions.content:
Trigonometric Ratios

SOH CAH TOA:

  • SOH: \(\sin \theta = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Hypotenuse}}\)
  • CAH: \(\cos \theta = \frac{\text{Adjacent}}{\text{Hypotenuse}}\)
  • TOA: \(\tan \theta = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}}\)

Example 1: Given that \(\tan \theta = \frac{4}{3}\), find \(\sin \theta\) and \(\cos \theta\).

Draw a right triangle: Opposite = 4, Adjacent = 3

\(\text{Hypotenuse} = \sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5\)

Therefore: \(\sin \theta = \frac{4}{5}\), \(\cos \theta = \frac{3}{5}\)

Example 2: Given that \(\sin \theta = \frac{8}{17}\), find \(\cos \theta\) and \(\tan \theta\).

In a right triangle with \(\sin \theta = \frac{8}{17}\): Opposite = 8, Hypotenuse = 17

\(\text{Adjacent} = \sqrt{17^2 - 8^2} = \sqrt{289 - 64} = \sqrt{225} = 15\)

Therefore: \(\cos \theta = \frac{15}{17}\), \(\tan \theta = \frac{8}{15}\)

Inverse Trigonometric Functions

Inverse trig functions do the opposite of the 'regular' trig functions:

  • \(\sin^{-1}\) (inverse sine) – does the opposite of sine
  • \(\cos^{-1}\) (inverse cosine) – does the opposite of cosine
  • \(\tan^{-1}\) (inverse tangent) – does the opposite of tangent

Note: \(\sin^{-1}(x)\) is not the same as \((\sin x)^{-1}\). The -1 simply means inverse function, not exponent.

sessions.title: Angles of Elevation and Depression
indicators: Apply trigonometry to solve problems involving angles of elevation and depression
sessions.content:
Angle of Elevation

The angle of elevation is the angle between the horizontal line of sight and the object when a person stands and looks up at an object.

Example: The angle of elevation of a plane at an altitude of 4500m is 27° to the ground. How far away is the plane?

\(\tan 27° = \frac{4500}{x}\)

\(x = \frac{4500}{\tan 27°} = \frac{4500}{0.5095} = 8832\) m

Angle of Depression

The angle of depression is the angle between the horizontal line of sight and the object when a person stands and looks down at an object.

Key point: The angle of elevation from one point to another is equal to the angle of depression from the other point back to the first (alternate angles).

Example: A cliff is 102m high. From the top of the cliff, the angle of depression of a boat is 30°. Find the distance of the boat from the foot of the cliff.

\(\tan 30° = \frac{102}{x}\)

\(x = \frac{102}{\tan 30°} = \frac{102}{0.5774} = 176.7\) m

sessions.title: Vector Notations and Types
indicators: Name vectors
Write the coordinates of a point as a vector
Identify the types of vectors
Find the magnitude of a vector
sessions.content:
Vector Quantity

A vector quantity is any quantity which has both magnitude and direction. Examples: displacement, acceleration, momentum, velocity, force.

A scalar quantity has magnitude only. Examples: time, speed, distance, mass, area.

Vector Notation

  • Column form: \(\overrightarrow{AB} = \begin{pmatrix} a \\ b \end{pmatrix}\) where \(a\) and \(b\) are the components
  • The component \(a\) is measured along the x-axis
  • The component \(b\) is measured along the y-axis
  • Magnitude and bearing form: (3km, 060°) – direction from the geographic north

Drawing a vector: Start from the point, move \(a\) units to the right (or left if negative) and \(b\) units upwards (or downwards if negative).

Types of Vectors

Zero vector: A vector with zero components \(\begin{pmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}\) – has zero magnitude.

Negative/Inverse of a vector: If \(\overrightarrow{AB} = \begin{pmatrix} a \\ b \end{pmatrix}\), then \(\overrightarrow{BA} = \begin{pmatrix} -a \\ -b \end{pmatrix}\). A vector and its inverse have the same magnitude but opposite directions.

Equality of vectors: Two vectors are equal if they have the same magnitude and direction. In terms of components, corresponding components must be the same.

Magnitude of a Vector

If \(\overrightarrow{AB} = \begin{pmatrix} a \\ b \end{pmatrix}\), then:

\(|\overrightarrow{AB}| = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}\)

Example: If \(\overrightarrow{PQ} = \begin{pmatrix} -8 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}\), find \(|\overrightarrow{PQ}|\).

\(|\overrightarrow{PQ}| = \sqrt{(-8)^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{64 + 4} = \sqrt{68} = 2\sqrt{17}\) units

sessions.title: Addition and Subtraction of Vectors
indicators: Add two or more vectors
Subtract two or more vectors
Multiply a scalar by a vector
sessions.content:
Addition and Subtraction of Vectors

To add vectors, add the corresponding components:

  • \(\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ y_1 \end{pmatrix}\), \(\mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} x_2 \\ y_2 \end{pmatrix}\)
  • \(\mathbf{a} + \mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} x_1 + x_2 \\ y_1 + y_2 \end{pmatrix}\)
  • \(\mathbf{a} - \mathbf{b} = \begin{pmatrix} x_1 - x_2 \\ y_1 - y_2 \end{pmatrix}\)

Example: If \(\mathbf{r} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 5 \end{pmatrix}\), \(\mathbf{s} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}\)

  • \(\mathbf{r} + \mathbf{s} = \begin{pmatrix} 4 \\ 9 \end{pmatrix}\)
  • \(\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{s} = \begin{pmatrix} -2 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix}\)

Resultant of Vectors

When two vectors are placed end to end, the vector that starts from the starting point of the first to the end point of the second is the resultant.

\(\overrightarrow{AB} + \overrightarrow{BC} = \overrightarrow{AC}\)

Multiplication of a Vector by a Scalar

If \(\overrightarrow{AB} = \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix}\), then \(k\overrightarrow{AB} = \begin{pmatrix} kx \\ ky \end{pmatrix}\) where \(k\) is a scalar.

  • When \(k\) is positive: The vectors are parallel and in the same direction
  • When \(k\) is negative: The vectors are parallel but in opposite directions
  • The length of the new vector is \(|k|\) times the length of the original vector

Example: If \(\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -2 \end{pmatrix}\), then \(3\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ -6 \end{pmatrix}\) and \(-2\mathbf{a} = \begin{pmatrix} -2 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}\)

sessions.title: Parallel, Perpendicular and Position Vectors
indicators: Tell whether two or more vectors are parallel
Tell whether two or more vectors are perpendicular
Determine if a vector is a position vector
sessions.content:
Parallel Vectors

Two non-zero vectors are parallel if one is a scalar multiple of the other.

  • If the scalar multiple is positive: Vectors are in the same direction
  • If the scalar multiple is negative: Vectors have opposite directions

Rule: If vector \(\begin{pmatrix} a \\ b \end{pmatrix}\) is parallel to vector \(\begin{pmatrix} c \\ d \end{pmatrix}\), then \(\frac{a}{c} = \frac{b}{d}\)

Example: \(\begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}\) and \(\begin{pmatrix} 6 \\ 12 \end{pmatrix}\) are parallel because \(\frac{2}{6} = \frac{4}{12} = \frac{1}{3}\)

Perpendicular Vectors

The perpendicular to a vector is found by rotating the vector through 90°.

If vector \(\begin{pmatrix} a \\ b \end{pmatrix}\), its perpendicular is \(\begin{pmatrix} -b \\ a \end{pmatrix}\) or \(\begin{pmatrix} b \\ -a \end{pmatrix}\) or their scalar multiples.

Rule: Two vectors \(\begin{pmatrix} a \\ b \end{pmatrix}\) and \(\begin{pmatrix} c \\ d \end{pmatrix}\) are perpendicular if \(a \times c + b \times d = 0\) (dot product = 0).

Position Vectors

Position vectors are denoted by small letters printed in bold type (e.g., \(\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}, \mathbf{p}, \mathbf{q}\)).

If point \(A\) is \((x, y)\) referenced to an origin \(O\), then \(\overrightarrow{OA} = \begin{pmatrix} x \\ y \end{pmatrix}\) is the position vector of \(A\).

Example: If \(A(3, -5)\), then \(\overrightarrow{OA} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 \\ -5 \end{pmatrix}\)

sessions.title: Finding the Vector Connecting Two Given Points and Their Mid-Points
indicators: Express the line joining two given points as a vector
Find the mid-point of a vector
sessions.content:
Vector Connecting Two Points

If \(A = (x_1, y_1)\) and \(B = (x_2, y_2)\) are two given points:

  • \(\overrightarrow{OA} = \begin{pmatrix} x_1 \\ y_1 \end{pmatrix}\), \(\overrightarrow{OB} = \begin{pmatrix} x_2 \\ y_2 \end{pmatrix}\)
  • \(\overrightarrow{AB} = \overrightarrow{OB} - \overrightarrow{OA} = \begin{pmatrix} x_2 - x_1 \\ y_2 - y_1 \end{pmatrix}\)

Note: Order of subtraction matters – \(\overrightarrow{AB}\) = position vector of B minus position vector of A.

Example: If \(A(1, 2)\) and \(B(3, 6)\), find \(\overrightarrow{AB}\).

\(\overrightarrow{AB} = \begin{pmatrix} 3 - 1 \\ 6 - 2 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix}\)

Mid-Point Theorem

If \(M\) is the mid-point of \(AB\) with position vectors \(\mathbf{a}\) and \(\mathbf{b}\) relative to the origin \(O\):

\(\overrightarrow{OM} = \frac{\mathbf{a} + \mathbf{b}}{2}\)

Thus, the position vector of the mid-point is half the sum of the position vectors of the endpoints.

Example: If \(A(1, 4)\) and \(B(-3, 2)\), find the position vector of the mid-point \(M\).

\(\overrightarrow{OM} = \frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 4 \end{pmatrix} + \begin{pmatrix} -3 \\ 2 \end{pmatrix}\right] = \frac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix} -2 \\ 6 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} -1 \\ 3 \end{pmatrix}\)

sessions.title: Bearings
indicators: Change vectors from magnitude-bearing form to component form
Solve real life problems involving bearing
sessions.content:
Bearings

A bearing is an angle measured clockwise from the north direction. Bearings are usually given in three digits (e.g., 025°, 135°, 270°).

The bearing of \(B\) from \(A\) is the angle measured clockwise from north at \(A\) to the line \(AB\).

The back bearing of \(B\) from \(A\) is the bearing of \(A\) from \(B\) (reverse direction).

  • Back bearing = Bearing + 180° (if bearing < 180°)
  • Back bearing = Bearing - 180° (if bearing > 180°)

Example: If the bearing of B from A is 045°, the bearing of A from B is 045° + 180° = 225°.

Changing Vectors from Magnitude-Bearing Form to Component Form

If a vector has magnitude \(v\) and the acute angle it makes with the x-axis is \(\theta\):

  • x-component = \(v \cos \theta\)
  • y-component = \(v \sin \theta\)

The appropriate sign must be attached depending on the quadrant:

  • Quadrant I: (+, +)
  • Quadrant II: (-, +)
  • Quadrant III: (-, -)
  • Quadrant IV: (+, -)

Example: An aircraft flies from A to B, 157 km away on a bearing of 135°. Find the components of \(\overrightarrow{AB}\).

Angle with x-axis = 180° - 135° = 45°

\(\overrightarrow{AB} = \begin{pmatrix} 157 \cos 45° \\ 157 \sin 45° \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 157 \times 0.707 \\ 157 \times 0.707 \end{pmatrix} = \begin{pmatrix} 111 \\ 111 \end{pmatrix}\)

sessions.title: Circle
indicators: Name parts of a circle
Find the circumference of a circle
Find the area of a circle
sessions.content:
Parts of a Circle

A circle is a plane figure bounded by a curved line such that the set of all points in the plane are at the same distance from a fixed point called the centre.

Key parts:

  • Circumference: The total distance around the circle (perimeter)
  • Radius (r): A line drawn from the centre to any point on the circumference
  • Diameter (d): A line which divides the circle into two equal parts – twice the radius: \(d = 2r\)
  • Chord: A straight line joining any two points on the circumference
  • Arc: Any portion of the circumference (minor arc and major arc)
  • Segment: The area bounded by a chord and the arc it cuts off
  • Sector: An area bounded by two radii and the arc between them
  • Semi-circle: Half of a circle
  • Quadrant: A quarter of the total area of a circle
  • Tangent: A straight line which touches a circle at only one point

Finding the Circumference of a Circle

Formula: \(C = 2\pi r\) or \(C = \pi d\)

where \(\pi = \frac{22}{7}\) (approximately 3.142)

Example 1: A circle has radius of 7cm. Find its circumference.

\(C = 2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 7 = 44\) cm

Example 2: A circle has diameter of 9mm. Find its circumference.

\(C = \frac{22}{7} \times 9 = 28.29\) mm

Circumference of a semi-circle: \(C = \frac{1}{2} \times 2\pi r = \pi r\)

In terms of diameter: \(C = \frac{1}{2} \times \pi d = \frac{\pi d}{2}\)

Finding the Area of a Circle

Formula: \(A = \pi r^2\) or \(A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}\)

Example: A circle has radius of 3cm. Find its area.

\(A = \frac{22}{7} \times 3^2 = \frac{22}{7} \times 9 = 28.29\) cm²

Area of a semi-circle: \(A = \frac{1}{2} \times \pi r^2\)

Area of a quadrant: \(A = \frac{1}{4} \times \pi r^2\)

sessions.title: Finding Arc Length and Area of Sector
indicators: Find the arc length of a circle
Find the area of a sector
sessions.content:
Finding the Length of an Arc

If an arc subtends an angle \(\theta\) at the centre of a circle with radius \(r\):

Arc length = \(\frac{\theta}{360} \times 2\pi r\)

Example 1: Calculate the length of an arc of a circle which subtends an angle of 60° at the centre of a circle with radius 7cm.

\(\text{Arc length} = \frac{60}{360} \times 2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 7 = \frac{1}{6} \times 44 = 7.33\) cm

Finding the Area of a Sector

Area of sector = \(\frac{\theta}{360} \times \pi r^2\)

Example 1: Calculate the area of a sector which subtends an angle of 60° at the centre of a circle with radius 7cm.

\(\text{Area} = \frac{60}{360} \times \frac{22}{7} \times 7^2 = \frac{1}{6} \times 154 = 25.67\) cm²

Example 2: An arc PQ subtends an angle of 100° at the centre of a circle with radius 7cm. Calculate the area of the:

  • Minor sector: \(\frac{100}{360} \times \frac{22}{7} \times 7^2 = 42.78\) cm²
  • Major sector: \(\frac{260}{360} \times \frac{22}{7} \times 7^2 = 111.22\) cm²

sessions.title: Lines of Symmetry and Total Surface Area of Cylinder
indicators: Find the line of symmetry of any given shape
Find the total surface area of a given shape
sessions.content:
Symmetry

A symmetrical figure is one that has a line that divides it in such a way that one half can be folded to fit exactly on the other.

Lines of symmetry for regular polygons:

  • Equilateral triangle: 3 lines
  • Isosceles triangle: 1 line
  • Scalene triangle: 0 lines
  • Square: 4 lines
  • Rectangle: 2 lines
  • Kite: 1 line
  • Rhombus: 2 lines
  • Parallelogram: 0 lines
  • Regular pentagon: 5 lines

Cylinder

A cylinder is a prism with a circular cross-section.

Volume of a cylinder: \(V = \pi r^2 h\)

Curved surface area of a cylinder: \(A = 2\pi r h\)

Total surface area of a closed cylinder: \(A = 2\pi r^2 + 2\pi r h = 2\pi r(r + h)\)

Total surface area of an open cylinder: \(A = \pi r^2 + 2\pi r h\)

Total surface area of a hollow cylinder: \(A = 2\pi r h\)

sessions.title: Volume and Surface Area of Triangular Prisms and Pyramids
indicators: Find the volume of triangular prisms and pyramids
sessions.content:
Triangular Prisms

A triangular prism has a triangular cross-section.

Volume of a triangular prism: \(V = A \times h\) where \(A\) is the cross-sectional area and \(h\) is the height of the prism.

For a triangular cross-section: \(A = \frac{1}{2} \times b \times h_t\) where \(b\) is the base of the triangle and \(h_t\) is the height of the triangle.

Volume: \(V = \frac{1}{2} \times b \times h_t \times h\)

Pyramids

Pyramids are named after the shape of their flat base:

  • Circular pyramids (cones)
  • Rectangular pyramids
  • Square pyramids
  • Triangular pyramids (tetrahedron)

Cone

Volume of a cone: \(V = \frac{1}{3} \times \pi r^2 h\)

Curved surface area of a cone: \(A = \pi r l\) where \(l\) is the slant height

Total surface area of a cone: \(A = \pi r^2 + \pi r l = \pi r(r + l)\)

Pythagorean relation in cone: \(l^2 = r^2 + h^2\)

Rectangular Pyramid

Volume of a rectangular pyramid: \(V = \frac{1}{3} \times A \times h\) where \(A\) is the area of the rectangular base.

Square Pyramid

Volume of a square pyramid: \(V = \frac{1}{3} \times s^2 \times h\) where \(s\) is the side of the square base.

Sphere

Surface area of a sphere: \(A = 4\pi r^2\)

Volume of a sphere: \(V = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3\)

sessions.title: Longitudes and Latitudes and Distances Between Them
indicators: Find the distance between two longitudes or latitudes
sessions.content:
Great and Small Circles

Great circle: A slice through the centre of a sphere – radius is the same as that of the sphere. Divides the sphere into two equal parts.

Small circle: Any other slice – radius is smaller than that of a great circle.

Latitude: Imaginary lines that run around the Earth – planes are perpendicular to the axis. The Equator is 0° – the only great circle among latitudes.

Longitude/Meridian: Circles passing through the North and South Poles – 'half' great circles that meet at the poles. The Prime Meridian is 0° (Greenwich Meridian).

Position coordinates: (\(\theta\)°N/S, \(\phi\)°E/W)

Distance Calculations

Along a great circle (longitude or equator):

\(\text{Distance} = \frac{\theta}{360} \times 2\pi R\)

where \(\theta\) is the angular distance and \(R\) is the radius of Earth (≈ 6370 km).

Along a small circle (latitude):

\(\text{Distance} = \frac{\theta}{360} \times 2\pi r\) where \(r = R \cos \alpha\)

and \(\alpha\) is the latitude.

Nautical miles: 1 nautical mile (nm) = 1.853 km. An arc of 1° subtends 60 nm.

\(\text{Distance in nm} = 60 \times \theta \times \cos \alpha\)

sessions.title: Distance Between Two Places on Latitudes
indicators: Find the distance between two places on the equator
sessions.content:
Distance Between Places on the Equator

The Equator is a great circle – use the great circle formula.

Example: Town A is at 15°E and town B is at 45°E on the Equator.

\(\theta = 45° - 15° = 30°\)

\(\text{Distance} = \frac{30}{360} \times 2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times 6400 = 3351\) km

Distance Between Places with the Same Latitude

For places on the same latitude \(\alpha\):

Radius of small circle: \(r = R \cos \alpha\)

Distance along latitude: \(\frac{\theta}{360} \times 2\pi r\)

where \(\theta\) is the difference in longitude.

Great circle distance (shortest route): Use the cosine rule to find the angle at the centre of the Earth, then calculate arc length.

Note: The great circle distance is shorter than the distance along the parallel of latitude.

sessions.title: Time Zones and Travel Times
indicators: Tell the time of a place with respect to another place
sessions.content:
Time Zones

The Earth spins through \(360°\) in 24 hours.

In one hour: \(360° \div 24 = 15°\)

In one degree: \(24 \times 60 \div 360 = 4\) minutes

Formulae:

  • For each \(15°\) difference in longitude = \(1\) hour difference in time
  • For each \(1°\) difference in longitude = \(4\) minutes difference in time
  • East is ahead of GMT; West is behind GMT

Calculating journey time:

\(\text{Journey time} = \text{Local arrival time} - \text{Local departure time} + \text{Time difference}\)

sessions.title: Types of Data
indicators: Identify types of statistics
Identify the types of data
Identify some methods of collecting data
sessions.content:
Statistics

Statistics is a branch of science which deals with the collection, classification, analysis, and interpretation of data to make informed decisions.

Two broad areas:

  • Descriptive statistics: Collection, organization, summarization, and presentation of data
  • Inferential statistics: Interpreting data to make predictions

Population: All observations or measurements of interest – the totality of all subjects possessing certain common traits.

Sample: A randomly selected number or group used to represent the entire population.

Random sample: Every element in the population has the same chance of being selected.

Data Collection Methods

  • Interview: Verbal interaction between questioner and respondent
  • Questionnaire: Written interaction
  • Observation: Researcher collects data by taking a closer look without asking questions
  • Experimentation: Performance of activities (measurement, mixing chemicals)
  • Tests: Class tests, assignments, quizzes
  • Past records: Compiling data from available records
  • Focus Group Discussion: Team of experts assembled to discuss an issue

Types of Data

Categorical Data: Represents characteristics (e.g., gender, language).

Nominal Data: Discrete units used to label variables with no quantitative value – no order (e.g., gender: Male/Female).

Ordinal Data: Discrete and ordered units – ordering matters (e.g., Educational level: Elementary, High School, Undergraduate, Graduate).

Numerical Data:

  • Discrete Data: Values are distinct and separate – can be counted (e.g., number of heads in coin flips)
  • Continuous Data: Represents measurements – values can't be counted but can be measured (e.g., height, weight)

Interval Data: Ordered units with same difference – no true zero (e.g., temperature).

Ratio Data: Ordered units with same difference – has an absolute zero (e.g., height, weight, length).

sessions.title: Presentation of Data in Table and Graph
indicators: Organize a given data in tabular form
Use the table to draw a graph
sessions.content:
Tabular Forms

Data can be presented as:

  • Array: Matrix of rows and columns of numbers arranged in order (ascending or descending)
  • Frequency distribution table: Data organized with tally marks and frequencies

Raw data: Data that are not organized.

Ungrouped frequency distribution: Values are listed individually with their frequencies.

Grouped frequency distribution: Values are grouped into classes (e.g., 0-9, 10-19, 20-29).

Key terms:

  • Class interval: The groups into which values are put (e.g., 30-39)
  • Class limits: End numbers of a class (lower class limit, upper class limit)
  • Class boundaries: Common points between classes – found by subtracting 0.5 from lower limits and adding 0.5 to upper limits
  • Class width/size: Difference between upper and lower class boundaries
  • Class mark: Mid-point of a class – \(\frac{\text{lower limit} + \text{upper limit}}{2}\)

Diagrammatic Representation

Bar chart: Rectangular bars of equal widths, lengths proportional to the magnitude of each item.

  • Simple bar chart: One bar per category
  • Component bar chart: Each bar is split into components
  • Multiple bar chart: Shows variation of two or more similar categories

Pie chart: Circular graph divided into sectors – each sector angle is proportional to the frequency.

\(\text{Sector angle} = \frac{\text{Value of category}}{\text{Total value}} \times 360°\)

Stem and leaf plot: Represents raw data without loss of information – 'leaves' are last digits, 'stems' are the remaining part.

Histogram: Graphical display of a frequency distribution – rectangular bars placed side by side.

Frequency polygon: Points joined by straight line segments at the mid-points of the tops of rectangles of a histogram.

Cumulative frequency (Ogive): Graphical method to present cumulative frequency – plotted against upper class boundaries.

sessions.title: Measures of Central Tendencies
indicators: Find the mode of a data
Find the median of a data
Find the mean of a data
sessions.content:
Mean (Arithmetic)

Mean: The sum of all values divided by the number of values.

\(\bar{x} = \frac{\sum x}{n}\) for raw data

\(\bar{x} = \frac{\sum fx}{\sum f}\) for grouped data

Assumed mean method: \(\bar{x} = A + \frac{\sum fd}{\sum f}\) where \(d = x - A\)

Median

Median: The middle score for a set of data arranged in order of magnitude.

  • Odd number of scores: The middle score
  • Even number of scores: The average of the two middle scores

For grouped data: Find the median class using \(\frac{N}{2}\), then use interpolation.

Median from cumulative frequency curve: Find \(\frac{N}{2}\) on the cumulative frequency axis, draw a horizontal line to the curve, then vertical to the variable axis.

Mode

Mode: The value which occurs most often in a set of data.

  • In a frequency distribution: The value with the highest frequency
  • In grouped data: The modal class – the class with the highest frequency

Bimodal: A distribution with two modes.

sessions.title: Measures of Dispersion
indicators: Find the range and interquartile range of a data
Find the deciles of a data
Find the percentiles of a data
Find the standard deviation of a data
Find the variance of a data
sessions.content:
Range

Range: The difference between the largest and smallest values in a data set.

\(\text{Range} = \text{Maximum} - \text{Minimum}\)

Interquartile Range (IQR)

Interquartile range: The difference between the third quartile and the first quartile.

\(\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1\)

Semi-interquartile range: \(\frac{Q_3 - Q_1}{2}\)

Percentiles and Deciles

Percentiles: Divide a set of observations into 100 equal parts.

For ungrouped data: The \(p\)th percentile is the value at position \(\frac{p}{100} \times (n+1)\) in the ordered data.

For grouped data: \(P_p = L + \frac{\frac{pn}{100} - cf}{f} \times h\)

where \(L\) = lower boundary, \(cf\) = cumulative frequency before, \(f\) = frequency of the class, \(h\) = class width.

Deciles: Divide data into 10 equal parts – \(D_1, D_2, ..., D_9\)

Mean Deviation

Mean deviation: The sum of the absolute values of the deviations from the mean divided by the number of items.

\(\text{MD} = \frac{\sum |x - \bar{x}|}{n}\)

Variance and Standard Deviation

Variance: The average of the squared deviations from the mean.

\(\sigma^2 = \frac{\sum (x - \bar{x})^2}{n}\)

Standard deviation: The positive square root of the variance.

\(\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (x - \bar{x})^2}{n}}\)

For grouped data:

\(\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum fx^2}{\sum f} - \left(\frac{\sum fx}{\sum f}\right)^2}\)

Using assumed mean: \(\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\sum fd^2}{\sum f} - \left(\frac{\sum fd}{\sum f}\right)^2}\)

sessions.title: Sample Space and Events
indicators: Explain what is meant by probability
Explain some terms associated with probability
sessions.content:
Meaning of Probability

Probability is the study of events which may or may not occur. The numerical value assigned to the likelihood of an event occurring is called the probability of the event.

Key Terms

Experiment: Any process that generates well-defined outcomes (e.g., tossing a coin, rolling a die).

Trial: Each repetition of an experiment.

Outcome: The possible result of trials of an experiment.

Equally likely: Each outcome has an equal chance of occurring.

Sample space (S): The set of all possible outcomes of an experiment. \(n(S)\) = number of elements in the sample space.

  • Tossing a coin: \(S = \{H, T\}\), \(n(S) = 2\)
  • Rolling a die: \(S = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}\), \(n(S) = 6\)
  • Tossing a coin 3 times: \(n(S) = 8\)

Event: A subset of the sample space. Denoted by capital letters (e.g., \(E, A, B\)). \(n(E)\) = number of events.

Simple event: An event consisting of a single point of the sample space.

Compound event: An event consisting of more than one single point of the sample space.

Probability measure (P): Assigns a probability to each event \(A\). Properties:

  • \(P(A)\) is always between 0 and 1 inclusive
  • \(P(\emptyset) = 0\)
  • \(P(S) = 1\)

sessions.title: Probability of Events
indicators: Find the probability of an event
sessions.content:
Calculating the Probability of an Event

Classical probability:

\(P(E) = \frac{n(E)}{n(S)} = \frac{\text{Number of ways E can occur}}{\text{Number of ways S can occur}}\)

Example 1: A fair die is tossed once. What is the probability that the number that shows up is 5?

\(S = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}\), \(n(S) = 6\), \(E = \{5\}\), \(n(E) = 1\)

\(P(5) = \frac{1}{6}\)

Example 2: What is the probability of getting an even number?

\(E = \{2, 4, 6\}\), \(n(E) = 3\)

\(P(\text{even}) = \frac{3}{6} = \frac{1}{2}\)

Experimental/Relative probability:

\(P(E) = \frac{\text{Number of times the outcome happens}}{\text{Total number of times the experiment is done}}\)

sessions.title: Addition Rule for Probability
indicators: Find the probability of two events that are mutually exclusive using addition rule
Find the probability of two events that are not mutually exclusive using addition rule
sessions.content:
Mutually Exclusive Events

Mutually exclusive events: The occurrence of one event excludes the occurrence of another event – they have no common outcomes.

Example: Rolling a die – events of getting a number less than 3 \(E_1 = \{1, 2\}\) and greater than 4 \(E_2 = \{5, 6\}\) are mutually exclusive.

Addition rule for mutually exclusive events:

\(P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B)\)

Not Mutually Exclusive Events

Not mutually exclusive events: The occurrence of one event includes the occurrence of another event – they have some common outcomes.

Addition rule for not mutually exclusive events:

\(P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)\)

Example: Drawing a card from a deck – probability of drawing a black card or a 6.

  • \(P(\text{black}) = \frac{26}{52} = \frac{1}{2}\)
  • \(P(6) = \frac{4}{52} = \frac{1}{13}\)
  • \(P(\text{black and 6}) = \frac{2}{52} = \frac{1}{26}\)
  • \(P(\text{black or 6}) = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{13} - \frac{1}{26} = \frac{7}{13}\)

sessions.title: Multiplication Rule for Probability
indicators: State the multiplication rule for probability
Use the multiplication rule for probability to solve related problems
sessions.content:
Independent Events

Independent events: The occurrence of one event is completely unaffected by the occurrence of another event.

Multiplication rule for independent events:

\(P(A \cap B) = P(A) \times P(B)\)

Example: Tossing a coin twice – probability of getting two consecutive heads.

\(P(H) = \frac{1}{2}\)

\(P(H \cap H) = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{4}\)

Dependent Events

Dependent events: The occurrence of one event affects the probability of the occurrence of another event (without replacement).

General multiplication rule:

\(P(A \cap B) = P(A) \times P(B|A)\)

where \(P(B|A)\) is the probability that B occurs given that A has occurred.

Example: Drawing two cards from a deck without replacement – probability of drawing a king then a queen.

  • \(P(\text{King}) = \frac{4}{52} = \frac{1}{13}\)
  • \(P(\text{Queen | King}) = \frac{4}{51}\)
  • \(P(\text{King} \cap \text{Queen}) = \frac{1}{13} \times \frac{4}{51} = \frac{4}{663}\)

Tree diagrams can be used to represent probability of independent and dependent events.

sessions.title: Complementary Events
indicators: Find the complementary event of a probability experiment
sessions.content:
Complementary Events

For any event \(E_1\), there exists another event \(E_1'\) which represents the remaining elements of the sample space \(S\).

\(E_1' = S - E_1\)

Complement Rule:

\(P(E') = 1 - P(E)\)

Key points:

  • The two events are mutually exclusive (you can't have both happen)
  • The two events are exhaustive (the sum of their probabilities is 1)

Example 1: Probability of getting a white ball from a bag is \(\frac{1}{4}\). What is the probability of not getting a white ball?

\(P(\text{not white}) = 1 - \frac{1}{4} = \frac{3}{4}\)

Example 2: Flipping a coin four times. What is the probability of getting at least one head?

  • Complement: Getting no heads = all tails
  • \(P(\text{all tails}) = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^4 = \frac{1}{16}\)
  • \(P(\text{at least one head}) = 1 - \frac{1}{16} = \frac{15}{16}\)
